title: Wild mushroom quiche
subtitle: Shortcrust tart with mixed mushrooms, gruyère, and thyme
excerpt: A deep, mushroom-heavy quiche in a shortcrust case: chestnut and oyster mushrooms fried with shallots, garlic, and thyme, joined by soaked dried porcini for depth, then set in a cream and egg custard with gruyère. The custard should be just set and trembling slightly at the centre when it comes out of the oven; it firms as it cools. Serve warm or at room temperature. It slices cleanly once it has rested for 20 minutes.
sourceNotes: Quiche is a dish of the Lorraine region of northeastern France, where quiche Lorraine (a cream and lardons custard in a shortcrust case) has been made since the sixteenth century. The mushroom variation belongs to the broader French tradition of savoury tarts and does not have a single regional origin. Deep quiches with wild mushrooms became standard on French and British restaurant menus from the 1970s. The addition of dried porcini alongside fresh mushrooms is a technique for adding depth associated with Elizabeth David's influence on British cooking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked and cooled. Reheat from frozen at 160 degrees C fan for 25 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case can be blind-baked 2 days ahead. The finished quiche keeps refrigerated for 3 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven for the custard; blind bake the case at 180 degrees C

glossaryTerms.definition: Baking a pastry case without filling, weighted down with baking beans or a similar substitute, so the base cooks through before the filling is added. The pastry is lined with parchment and filled with beans to prevent it puffing up. The case is then baked empty for a further few minutes once the beans are removed, to dry out the base. Wet fillings — cream custards, fruit compotes — make an unbaked pastry base soggy; blind baking prevents this.

content.text: before filling. A raw case under a cream custard produces a raw, pale base. After blind baking, the mushrooms go in first as a layer, then the custard is poured over. The custard is done when it is just set at the edges with a slight wobble in the centre; the heat of the case continues cooking it off the oven.

items.prepNote: soaked in 100 ml hot water for 20 minutes, drained and chopped (reserve soaking liquid)

content.text: Make and blind bake the pastry case

Rub 100 g cold butter into 200 g flour and 0.25 tsp salt until it resembles fine breadcrumbs. Mix in 1 egg yolk and 2 to 3 tablespoons of cold water to form a dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll out and line a 23 cm tart tin with a loose base. Press into the fluted edges and trim, leaving a small overhang. Refrigerate for 15 minutes. Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Line the case with parchment and fill with baking beans. Bake for 15 minutes. Remove beans and paper. Bake for a further 8 minutes until dry and just beginning to colour. Lower the oven to 165 degrees C fan.

Melt 30 g butter in a large pan over high heat. Add the shallots and cook for 2 minutes. Add the chestnut mushrooms in a single layer and cook without stirring for 3 minutes. Stir, add the oyster mushrooms, garlic, and thyme leaves. Cook for 4 to 5 minutes until all moisture has evaporated. Stir in the soaked porcini and 0.5 tsp salt. Cool slightly.

Scatter the mushroom filling over the blind-baked case. Whisk 300 ml double cream with 3 eggs, 2 egg yolks, 100 g grated gruyère, 0.5 tsp salt, and 0.5 tsp black pepper. Pour over the mushrooms. Bake at 165 degrees C fan for 30 to 35 minutes until the custard is just set with a slight wobble in the centre. Rest for 20 minutes before removing from the tin and slicing.

items.problem: Custard is watery and has not set
items.solution: The oven temperature was too low or the quiche was underbaked. The custard should tremble very slightly in the centre but not slosh when the tin is tilted. Return to the oven for 5-minute intervals until set.
The blind bake was insufficient, or the mushrooms were added without cooking off their liquid first. Mushrooms must be cooked until the pan is dry before they go into the case.
The oven was too hot and the egg cooked too quickly. Reduce to 155 degrees C and check earlier. Quiche custard is cooked low and slow — it cracks if rushed.
